answer:
Designing a Practical ES6 Modules Architecture for a Mid-Size Product

1. Definition & Context

ES6 Modules (ESM) are JavaScript's native module system, using import and export statements to organize code into reusable, encapsulated units. Unlike CommonJS (require), ESM is statically analyzable, enabling tree-shaking by bundlers, strict mode by default, and top-level await. For a mid-size product, a well-structured module graph prevents circular dependencies, enables code-splitting, and makes the codebase navigable for a growing team.

2. Real Project Use-Case — HR Leave Management System

In Manoj's HR Leave Management System, the Fastify backend needed a clean module architecture to separate concerns across authentication, leave logic, notification, and database access. Manoj organized the project using a barrel-export pattern with layered modules:

  • src/modules/auth/ — JWT verification, role guards, token refresh logic
  • src/modules/leave/ — leave application, approval chain, balance calculation
  • src/modules/notification/ — Socket.io event emitters, email dispatchers
  • src/shared/ — Prisma client singleton, validation schemas (Zod), utility functions

Each module folder had an index.ts barrel file that re-exported only the public API, keeping internal helpers private. The Angular 18 frontend mirrored this with feature modules using lazy-loaded routes, where each feature imported only what it needed from a shared library barrel.

3. Module Architecture Diagram

4. Implementation Code

// shared/index.ts — Barrel export (public API only)
export { prisma } from './prisma.js';
export { leaveSchema, employeeSchema } from './schemas.js';
export { formatDate, paginate } from './utils.js';
export type { PaginatedResult, LeaveStatus } from './types.js';
// NOTE: internal helpers like dbRetry, hashPassword are NOT exported

// shared/prisma.ts — Singleton Prisma client
import { PrismaClient } from '@prisma/client';

const globalForPrisma = globalThis as unknown as { prisma: PrismaClient };
export const prisma = globalForPrisma.prisma ?? new PrismaClient({
  log: process.env.NODE_ENV === 'development' ? ['query'] : [],
});
if (process.env.NODE_ENV !== 'production') globalForPrisma.prisma = prisma;

// modules/leave/index.ts — Public API barrel
export { leaveRoutes } from './leave.routes.js';
export { LeaveService } from './leave.service.js';
// leave.repository.ts is internal — not exported

// modules/leave/leave.service.ts
import { prisma, leaveSchema } from '../../shared/index.js';
import type { LeaveStatus } from '../../shared/index.js';
import { emitEvent } from '../notification/index.js';

export class LeaveService {
  async applyLeave(employeeId: string, data: unknown) {
    const validated = leaveSchema.parse(data);
    const leave = await prisma.leaveRequest.create({
      data: { ...validated, employeeId, status: 'pending' as LeaveStatus },
    });
    // Notify manager via Socket.io
    await emitEvent('leave:new', { leaveId: leave.id, employeeId });
    return leave;
  }

  async approveLeave(leaveId: string, managerId: string) {
    const leave = await prisma.leaveRequest.update({
      where: { id: leaveId },
      data: { status: 'approved' as LeaveStatus, approvedBy: managerId },
    });
    await emitEvent('leave:approved', { leaveId, employeeId: leave.employeeId });
    return leave;
  }
}

// app.ts — Entry point wiring
import Fastify from 'fastify';
import { leaveRoutes } from './modules/leave/index.js';
import { authRoutes } from './modules/auth/index.js';
import { notifyPlugin } from './modules/notification/index.js';

const app = Fastify({ logger: true });
app.register(notifyPlugin);
app.register(authRoutes, { prefix: '/api/auth' });
app.register(leaveRoutes, { prefix: '/api/leave' });
app.listen({ port: 3000 });

5. Debugging Narrative — Manoj's Experience

While building the HR Leave System, Manoj encountered a circular dependency between leave/leave.service.ts and notification/notification.service.ts. The leave service imported the notification service to emit events on approval, and the notification service imported the leave service to fetch leave details for email templates. At runtime, one of the imports resolved to undefined, causing a TypeError: Cannot read property 'getLeaveById' of undefined.

Manoj diagnosed it by adding console.log at the top of each module to trace the import order. He saw that notification.service.ts loaded first, but LeaveService was still undefined at that point because ESM evaluates modules in dependency order. The fix was to break the cycle with dependency inversion: the notification service accepted a getLeaveById function parameter instead of importing the leave module directly, and this function was injected during app startup in app.ts.

6. Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallMitigation
Circular dependencies between feature modules cause undefined imports at runtimeUse dependency inversion — accept dependencies as constructor/function parameters; use a DI container or manual wiring in the entry point
Barrel files re-export too much, bloating bundle sizeOnly export public API from barrel; use sideEffects: false in package.json to enable tree-shaking; audit with npx webpack-bundle-analyzer
Mixing CJS require() with ESM import causes ERR_REQUIRE_ESMSet "type": "module" in package.json consistently; use .cjs extension for any CJS config files (e.g., jest.config.cjs)
Forgetting .js extension in relative imports breaks Node.js ESM resolutionConfigure TypeScript with "moduleResolution": "NodeNext" which enforces explicit extensions; add an ESLint rule import/extensions

7. Validation Strategy

  • Circular dependency check: Run npx madge --circular src/ in CI to detect import cycles before merge
  • Bundle analysis: Verify tree-shaking works — import one function from shared/, assert the bundle does not include unrelated exports
  • Module isolation test: Each module can be imported independently without side effects — import { LeaveService } from './modules/leave/index.js' does not trigger database connections
  • Playwright E2E: Verify lazy-loaded Angular feature modules only download when the user navigates to that route (check Network waterfall)

Debugging a Real ES6 Modules Issue in an Interview Context

1. Definition & Context

Debugging ES6 Module issues requires understanding the module resolution algorithm, the difference between static and dynamic imports, and how the JavaScript runtime (Node.js or browser) resolves module specifiers. Common issues include circular dependencies producing undefined exports, missing file extensions in Node.js ESM mode, CJS/ESM interop failures, and stale module caches during development. In an interview, demonstrating systematic diagnosis of these issues signals deep JavaScript knowledge.

2. Real Project Use-Case — ContactsHub

In Manoj's ContactsHub project (React 18 + Fastify + MongoDB), he migrated the Fastify backend from CommonJS to ES Modules to enable top-level await for database initialization. After setting "type": "module" in package.json, the server crashed on startup with ERR_REQUIRE_ESM — a third-party middleware package (express-fileupload) was being imported with import but internally used module.exports, and Node.js refused to load it as ESM.

3. Diagnostic Flow Diagram

4. Debugging Code

// BEFORE — broken: CJS package imported in ESM context
// package.json: { "type": "module" }
import fileUpload from 'express-fileupload'; // ERR_REQUIRE_ESM!
import Fastify from 'fastify';

const app = Fastify();
app.register(fileUpload); // never reaches here

// WORKAROUND (not ideal) — createRequire for CJS interop
import { createRequire } from 'module';
const require = createRequire(import.meta.url);
const fileUpload = require('express-fileupload'); // works but loses types

// AFTER — proper fix: use ESM-native Fastify plugin
import Fastify from 'fastify';
import multipart from '@fastify/multipart'; // native ESM support

const app = Fastify({ logger: true });
await app.register(multipart, {
  limits: { fileSize: 5 * 1024 * 1024 }, // 5 MB
});

// Route using Fastify multipart (ESM-compatible)
app.post('/api/contacts/avatar', async (req, reply) => {
  const file = await req.file();
  if (!file) return reply.code(400).send({ error: 'No file uploaded' });
  
  const buffer = await file.toBuffer();
  const url = await uploadToCloudinary(buffer, file.filename);
  return { avatarUrl: url };
});

// Also fixed: missing .js extensions in relative imports
// BEFORE (broken in Node ESM):
import { prisma } from '../shared/prisma';      // ERR_MODULE_NOT_FOUND
// AFTER (correct):
import { prisma } from '../shared/prisma.js';   // works

// tsconfig.json fix for TypeScript + ESM
// {
//   "compilerOptions": {
//     "module": "NodeNext",
//     "moduleResolution": "NodeNext",
//     "outDir": "./dist",
//     "rootDir": "./src"
//   }
// }

await app.listen({ port: 3000 });
console.log('Server started in ESM mode');

5. Debugging Narrative — Manoj's Experience

Manoj decided to migrate ContactsHub's backend to ESM to use top-level await for the MongoDB connection. The migration seemed straightforward — change package.json to "type": "module" and switch require to import. But it broke in three places:

  1. First error: ERR_REQUIRE_ESM on express-fileupload. Manoj checked the package's package.json — no "exports" field, only "main": "lib/index.js" with module.exports. He replaced it with @fastify/multipart which had proper ESM exports.
  2. Second error: ERR_MODULE_NOT_FOUND on every relative import. Node.js ESM requires explicit .js extensions. Manoj used a find-and-replace regex to add .js to all 47 relative imports.
  3. Third error: Jest tests failed because Jest's ESM support was experimental. Manoj switched to vitest which natively supports ESM, and all 32 tests passed without configuration changes.

Total migration time: 3 hours. The payoff was cleaner async initialization and future-proof module compatibility.

6. Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallMitigation
ERR_REQUIRE_ESM when importing CJS packages in ESM contextCheck package's exports field before depending on it; prefer packages with "type": "module"; use createRequire as a temporary bridge
Missing .js extensions in relative imports cause ERR_MODULE_NOT_FOUNDSet "moduleResolution": "NodeNext" in tsconfig — TypeScript will error at compile time if extensions are missing
__dirname and __filename are undefined in ESMUse import.meta.url with new URL('.', import.meta.url).pathname or the fileURLToPath utility from node:url
Jest does not fully support ESM without experimental flagsMigrate to vitest which has native ESM support, or use --experimental-vm-modules flag with Jest 29+

7. Validation Strategy

  • Startup smoke test: CI script that boots the server in ESM mode and verifies it responds to a health check within 5 seconds
  • Import lint: ESLint rule import/extensions: ['error', 'always'] enforces explicit .js extensions on all relative imports
  • CJS interop audit: Script that scans node_modules for packages lacking ESM exports and flags them before migration
  • Circular dependency CI gate: npx madge --circular --extensions ts src/ fails the build if any import cycle is detected

Designing a Practical Closures Implementation for a Mid-Size Product

1. Definition & Context

A closure is a function that retains access to variables from its enclosing lexical scope, even after the outer function has returned. In JavaScript, every function forms a closure over its creation context. Closures power fundamental patterns like data privacy, factory functions, memoization, partial application, and event handler state management. For a mid-size product, closures are not just a language curiosity — they are the mechanism behind module-private state, callback context retention, and efficient caching strategies.

2. Real Project Use-Case — CRM Sales Dashboard

In Manoj's CRM Sales Dashboard (Angular 18 + Express.js + MongoDB), closures were used in three critical areas:

  • Rate limiter factory: A closure-based rate limiter in the Express.js middleware that captured per-IP counters in a closed-over Map, avoiding global variables or external state stores for simple rate limiting.
  • Memoized aggregation: A closure that cached expensive MongoDB aggregation results (pipeline value by stage), returning cached data if called within a configurable TTL window.
  • Event handler cleanup: Angular component subscriptions wrapped in closures that captured the subscription reference, enabling automatic cleanup in ngOnDestroy without leaking references.

3. Closure Mechanics Diagram

4. Implementation Code

// 1. Rate limiter factory using closures (CRM Express.js backend)
function createRateLimiter(maxReqs: number, windowMs: number) {
  // These variables are closed over — private to this limiter instance
  const hits = new Map<string, { count: number; resetAt: number }>();

  // Cleanup expired entries periodically
  setInterval(() => {
    const now = Date.now();
    for (const [ip, entry] of hits) {
      if (now > entry.resetAt) hits.delete(ip);
    }
  }, windowMs);

  // The returned middleware closes over hits, maxReqs, windowMs
  return (req: Request, res: Response, next: NextFunction) => {
    const ip = req.ip ?? 'unknown';
    const now = Date.now();
    const entry = hits.get(ip);

    if (!entry || now > entry.resetAt) {
      hits.set(ip, { count: 1, resetAt: now + windowMs });
      return next();
    }

    if (entry.count < maxReqs) {
      entry.count++;
      return next();
    }

    res.status(429).json({
      error: 'Too many requests',
      retryAfter: Math.ceil((entry.resetAt - now) / 1000),
    });
  };
}

// Usage: each route gets its own closure with independent state
app.use('/api/leads', createRateLimiter(100, 60_000));     // 100/min
app.use('/api/deals/export', createRateLimiter(5, 300_000)); // 5 per 5min

// 2. Memoization closure for expensive aggregation
function createMemoizedQuery<T>(
  queryFn: () => Promise<T>,
  ttlMs: number
) {
  let cachedResult: T | null = null;
  let cachedAt = 0;

  return async (): Promise<T> => {
    const now = Date.now();
    if (cachedResult && now - cachedAt < ttlMs) {
      return cachedResult; // serve from closure-held cache
    }
    cachedResult = await queryFn();
    cachedAt = now;
    return cachedResult;
  };
}

// Usage: pipeline stats cached for 30 seconds
const getPipelineStats = createMemoizedQuery(
  () => Deal.aggregate([
    { $group: { _id: '$stage', total: { $sum: '$value' } } },
  ]),
  30_000
);

app.get('/api/dashboard/pipeline', async (req, res) => {
  const stats = await getPipelineStats(); // closure manages cache
  res.json(stats);
});

5. Debugging Narrative — Manoj's Experience

In the CRM Dashboard, Manoj noticed that the rate limiter for /api/deals/export was blocking legitimate users after server restarts. Investigation revealed the issue was not in the closure logic itself but in an interaction with the closure state: the setInterval cleanup kept running even after the Express server was gracefully shut down during deployment, and the hits Map was garbage collected on restart, so the first requests after restart got unfairly rate-limited by a stale timer from a previous module load (during hot-reload in development).

Manoj fixed it by returning a cleanup function from the factory: return { middleware, cleanup: () => clearInterval(timerId) }. In the server shutdown hook (process.on('SIGTERM')), he called cleanup() to stop the interval. This also fixed a subtle memory leak where the Map was held in memory by the orphaned setInterval callback.

6. Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallMitigation
Closures holding references to large objects prevent garbage collection (memory leaks)Nullify references when no longer needed; use WeakRef or WeakMap for cache entries; profile with --inspect heap snapshots
Stale closures in React: event handlers capture old state valuesUse useRef for mutable current values; use functional state updates setState(prev => ...); add dependencies to useEffect
Closures inside loops capture the same variable reference (classic var bug)Always use let or const in loops (block-scoped); avoid var entirely with ESLint no-var rule
Timer callbacks (setInterval) keep closure scope alive indefinitelyStore interval IDs and clear them on shutdown/component unmount; return cleanup functions from factory closures

7. Validation Strategy

  • Unit test factory isolation: Create two rate limiters from the same factory, verify they maintain independent state (requests to limiter A don't affect limiter B)
  • Memory leak detection: Use process.memoryUsage() before/after 10,000 closure creations; assert heap growth is bounded (closures are GC'd when references are released)
  • Memoization correctness: Call memoized function twice within TTL, assert DB query executes only once (mock the query function and check call count)
  • Cleanup verification: After calling cleanup(), verify the setInterval is cleared by checking that no more Map entries are deleted after the window expires

Debugging a Real Closures Issue in an Interview Context

1. Definition & Context

Debugging closure issues requires understanding what variables a function captures and when those variables are evaluated. The most common closure bugs are: (1) stale closures where a function captures an outdated value, (2) unintended variable sharing in loops, (3) memory leaks from closures retaining references to large scopes, and (4) the this context being lost in closures (pre-arrow-function patterns). In an interview, walking through a closure bug demonstrates mastery of JavaScript's scoping and execution model.

2. Real Project Use-Case — ContactsHub

In Manoj's ContactsHub React 18 app, a subtle stale closure bug appeared in the contact search feature. The search input used a debounced callback to fetch filtered contacts from the Fastify API. After typing quickly and then clearing the search, the contact list showed stale filtered results instead of the full list. The debounced function had captured the old search term in its closure, and when it finally fired, it sent the outdated query string to the API.

3. Diagnostic Flow Diagram

4. Debugging Code

// BEFORE — stale closure bug in ContactsHub search
function ContactSearch() {
  const [query, setQuery] = useState('');
  const [contacts, setContacts] = useState([]);

  // BUG: debounced function captures 'query' at creation time
  const debouncedSearch = useMemo(
    () =>
      debounce(async () => {
        // 'query' here is the value when useMemo ran, NOT current value
        const res = await fetch(`/api/contacts?search=${query}`);
        const data = await res.json();
        setContacts(data);
      }, 300),
    [] // empty deps — 'query' is always the initial ''
  );

  useEffect(() => {
    debouncedSearch();
  }, [query]);

  return <input value={query} onChange={e => setQuery(e.target.value)} />;
}

// AFTER — fixed with useRef + AbortController
function ContactSearch() {
  const [query, setQuery] = useState('');
  const [contacts, setContacts] = useState([]);
  const queryRef = useRef(query);
  const abortRef = useRef<AbortController | null>(null);

  // Keep ref in sync with latest state
  useEffect(() => {
    queryRef.current = query;
  }, [query]);

  const debouncedSearch = useMemo(
    () =>
      debounce(async () => {
        // Cancel any in-flight request
        abortRef.current?.abort();
        abortRef.current = new AbortController();

        try {
          // Read from ref — always gets the LATEST value
          const currentQuery = queryRef.current;
          const res = await fetch(
            `/api/contacts?search=${encodeURIComponent(currentQuery)}`,
            { signal: abortRef.current.signal }
          );
          const data = await res.json();
          setContacts(data);
        } catch (err) {
          if (err instanceof DOMException && err.name === 'AbortError') {
            return; // request was cancelled, ignore
          }
          throw err;
        }
      }, 300),
    [] // safe: reads from ref, not stale closure
  );

  useEffect(() => {
    debouncedSearch();
    return () => abortRef.current?.abort(); // cleanup on unmount
  }, [query]);

  return (
    <input
      value={query}
      onChange={e => setQuery(e.target.value)}
      placeholder="Search contacts..."
    />
  );
}

5. Debugging Narrative — Manoj's Experience

Manoj noticed the bug during Playwright E2E testing of ContactsHub. The test typed "john" into the search box, verified 3 results appeared, then cleared the input and expected all 50 contacts. Instead, the list flickered — briefly showing 50 contacts, then reverting to 3 (the stale "john" results).

  1. Observation: Added console.log('fetching:', query) inside the debounced function. After clearing, it logged fetching: john — the stale captured value.
  2. Diagnosis: The useMemo had empty dependency array, so the closure captured query = '' from the first render. But even if deps included query, the debounced function would still be recreated on every keystroke, defeating the debounce.
  3. Solution: Used useRef to always read the latest value without recreating the debounced function. Added AbortController to cancel stale in-flight requests.
  4. Verification: Playwright test passed — type "john", see 3 results, clear input, see all 50 contacts with no flicker.

6. Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallMitigation
Stale closures in React hooks capture old state/prop valuesUse useRef for values that need to be current inside callbacks; use functional setState(prev => ...) instead of reading state directly
Debounced functions with stale closures cause race conditions with API responsesCombine useRef for latest value + AbortController to cancel superseded requests
Adding state to useMemo/useCallback deps recreates the function, breaking debounce/throttleDecouple: create the debounced function once (empty deps) and read mutable current values from refs
Closure-based timers (setTimeout) fire with captured values after component re-rendersClear timers in useEffect cleanup; use useRef to track the timer ID; cancel on unmount

7. Validation Strategy

  • Playwright race condition test: Rapidly type "john", clear, type "jane", clear — assert final list shows all contacts, no stale results
  • React Testing Library: Mock fetch with delays; fire rapid input changes; assert only the last fetch result is rendered
  • AbortController verification: Mock fetch, fire 5 rapid searches, assert fetch was called 5 times but abort() was called 4 times (all but the last)
  • Memory profiling: Mount/unmount component 100 times; assert no dangling fetch handlers or timer references via heap snapshot comparison

Designing a Practical Promises Implementation for a Mid-Size Product

1. Definition & Context

A Promise represents the eventual completion (or failure) of an asynchronous operation and its resulting value. Promises provide a structured alternative to callbacks, enabling chaining (.then), error propagation (.catch), and concurrent execution (Promise.all, Promise.allSettled, Promise.race). For a mid-size product, practical Promise usage goes beyond basic async/await — it involves patterns for parallel data fetching, graceful degradation, retry logic, timeout enforcement, and cancellation via AbortController.

2. Real Project Use-Case — HR Leave Management System

In Manoj's HR Leave Management System, the leave approval workflow required multiple asynchronous operations that had to execute with specific concurrency semantics:

  • Promise.all: When a manager approved a leave, the system simultaneously updated the leave status in PostgreSQL, decremented the employee's balance, and sent a Socket.io notification — all three had to succeed or the transaction was rolled back.
  • Promise.allSettled: The dashboard loaded data from 4 independent sources (leave stats, team calendar, pending approvals, announcements). Using allSettled, if the announcements API was down, the dashboard still rendered the other 3 sections.
  • Promise.race with timeout: External email API calls were wrapped in a race against a 5-second timeout to prevent slow email delivery from blocking the approval response.

3. Promise Concurrency Patterns Diagram

4. Implementation Code

// 1. Promise.all — Transactional leave approval (HR Leave System)
import { prisma } from '../shared/index.js';
import { emitEvent } from '../modules/notification/index.js';

async function approveLeave(leaveId: string, managerId: string) {
  // Use Prisma transaction + Promise.all for atomicity
  return prisma.$transaction(async (tx) => {
    const leave = await tx.leaveRequest.update({
      where: { id: leaveId },
      data: { status: 'approved', approvedBy: managerId },
    });

    // All three must succeed inside the transaction
    await Promise.all([
      tx.leaveBalance.update({
        where: { employeeId: leave.employeeId },
        data: { remaining: { decrement: leave.days } },
      }),
      tx.auditLog.create({
        data: {
          action: 'leave_approved',
          actorId: managerId,
          targetId: leaveId,
        },
      }),
      emitEvent('leave:approved', {
        leaveId,
        employeeId: leave.employeeId,
      }),
    ]);

    return leave;
  });
}

// 2. Promise.allSettled — Resilient dashboard loading
async function loadDashboard(userId: string) {
  const [stats, calendar, pending, announcements] =
    await Promise.allSettled([
      fetchLeaveStats(userId),
      fetchTeamCalendar(userId),
      fetchPendingApprovals(userId),
      fetchAnnouncements(), // might be down
    ]);

  return {
    stats: stats.status === 'fulfilled' ? stats.value : null,
    calendar: calendar.status === 'fulfilled' ? calendar.value : null,
    pending: pending.status === 'fulfilled' ? pending.value : [],
    announcements:
      announcements.status === 'fulfilled'
        ? announcements.value
        : { error: 'Service unavailable', items: [] },
    degraded: [stats, calendar, pending, announcements].some(
      (r) => r.status === 'rejected'
    ),
  };
}

// 3. Promise.race with timeout — Email with deadline
function withTimeout<T>(promise: Promise<T>, ms: number): Promise<T> {
  const timeout = new Promise<never>((_, reject) =>
    setTimeout(() => reject(new Error(`Timeout after ${ms}ms`)), ms)
  );
  return Promise.race([promise, timeout]);
}

async function sendApprovalEmail(to: string, leaveId: string) {
  try {
    await withTimeout(
      emailService.send({
        to,
        subject: 'Leave Approved',
        template: 'leave-approved',
        data: { leaveId },
      }),
      5000 // 5-second deadline
    );
    return { sent: true };
  } catch (err) {
    // Log but don't fail the approval
    console.error('Email failed or timed out:', err);
    return { sent: false, reason: (err as Error).message };
  }
}

// 4. Retry with exponential backoff
async function withRetry<T>(
  fn: () => Promise<T>,
  maxRetries = 3,
  baseDelayMs = 200
): Promise<T> {
  for (let attempt = 0; attempt <= maxRetries; attempt++) {
    try {
      return await fn();
    } catch (err) {
      if (attempt === maxRetries) throw err;
      const delay = baseDelayMs * Math.pow(2, attempt);
      await new Promise((r) => setTimeout(r, delay));
    }
  }
  throw new Error('Unreachable');
}

5. Debugging Narrative — Manoj's Experience

In the HR Leave System, Manoj encountered a critical production issue: leave approvals were intermittently failing silently. The manager clicked "Approve", the UI showed success (optimistic update), but the database remained unchanged. The root cause was an unhandled Promise rejection inside Promise.all.

The Socket.io emitEvent function occasionally threw when the employee's socket session had expired. Because it was inside Promise.all, the entire group rejected, but the .catch on the route handler sent a generic 500 error that the Angular interceptor swallowed (it retried once, then gave up silently).

Manoj fixed it in three steps:

  1. Separated critical operations (DB update, balance decrement) from non-critical ones (Socket.io notification). DB operations stayed in Promise.all inside the Prisma transaction; the notification was moved outside with its own try/catch.
  2. Added process.on('unhandledRejection') to log any escaped Promise rejections.
  3. Added a Fastify onError hook that logged the full error chain, making silent failures visible.

6. Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallMitigation
Promise.all fails entirely if one promise rejects — losing results from successful onesUse Promise.allSettled for independent operations; reserve Promise.all for operations that must all succeed (transactions)
Unhandled rejections crash Node.js 18+ by default (--unhandled-rejections=throw)Always .catch() or use try/catch with async/await; add process.on('unhandledRejection') for safety net logging
Promise.race timeout doesn't cancel the losing promise — it keeps running in the backgroundPair Promise.race with AbortController: pass signal to the actual operation so it's truly cancelled when the timeout wins
Sequential awaits in a loop (for...of with await) serialize operations that could be parallelCollect promises into an array first, then await Promise.all(promises); use p-map for controlled concurrency

7. Validation Strategy

  • Transaction atomicity test: Mock Socket.io to throw; verify DB changes are rolled back and the leave status is still "pending"
  • Partial failure test: In dashboard loader, mock one API to reject; verify the response includes degraded: true and the other 3 sections are populated
  • Timeout test: Mock email service with a 10-second delay; verify withTimeout(5000) rejects in ~5 seconds and the approval response returns within 6 seconds
  • Retry test: Mock a function that fails twice then succeeds; verify withRetry returns the result after 3 attempts with exponential delays

Debugging a Real Promises Issue in an Interview Context

1. Definition & Context

Debugging Promise issues requires understanding the microtask queue, Promise state transitions (pending → fulfilled/rejected), error propagation through chains, and common anti-patterns like missing return in .then() chains, fire-and-forget async calls, and await inside loops. In an interview, demonstrating diagnosis of a real Promise bug shows you understand JavaScript's async execution model at a practical level — not just theory.

2. Real Project Use-Case — ContactsHub

In Manoj's ContactsHub (React 18 + Fastify + MongoDB), the bulk CSV import feature allowed users to upload a CSV file with up to 500 contacts. The import endpoint parsed the CSV, validated each row, and inserted contacts into MongoDB. The initial implementation used a for...of loop with await on each insert — and users reported that importing 500 contacts took 45 seconds. Worse, if any single insert failed (e.g., duplicate email), the entire import stopped mid-way, leaving a partial import with no indication of which rows succeeded.

3. Diagnostic Flow Diagram

4. Debugging Code

// BEFORE — sequential awaits: 45 seconds for 500 contacts
app.post('/api/contacts/import', async (req, reply) => {
  const rows = parseCSV(req.body.csv);
  const results = [];

  for (const row of rows) {
    try {
      // BUG: each insert awaited individually = sequential execution
      const contact = await Contact.create({
        name: row.name,
        email: row.email,
        phone: row.phone,
        userId: req.headers['x-user-id'],
      });
      results.push({ email: row.email, status: 'ok' });
    } catch (err) {
      // BUG: stops on first error, remaining rows never processed
      return reply.code(500).send({
        error: `Failed at row ${results.length + 1}`,
      });
    }
  }

  return { imported: results.length };
});

// AFTER — chunked Promise.allSettled: ~1.2 seconds
function chunk<T>(arr: T[], size: number): T[][] {
  const chunks: T[][] = [];
  for (let i = 0; i < arr.length; i += size) {
    chunks.push(arr.slice(i, i + size));
  }
  return chunks;
}

app.post('/api/contacts/import', async (req, reply) => {
  const rows = parseCSV(req.body.csv);
  const userId = req.headers['x-user-id'] as string;
  const batchSize = 50;
  const batches = chunk(rows, batchSize);

  const allResults: Array<{
    email: string;
    status: 'created' | 'failed';
    error?: string;
  }> = [];

  for (const batch of batches) {
    // Each batch runs in parallel; batches run sequentially
    // to avoid overwhelming MongoDB connection pool
    const settled = await Promise.allSettled(
      batch.map((row) =>
        Contact.create({
          name: row.name,
          email: row.email,
          phone: row.phone,
          userId,
        })
      )
    );

    settled.forEach((result, idx) => {
      if (result.status === 'fulfilled') {
        allResults.push({ email: batch[idx].email, status: 'created' });
      } else {
        allResults.push({
          email: batch[idx].email,
          status: 'failed',
          error: result.reason?.message ?? 'Unknown error',
        });
      }
    });
  }

  const created = allResults.filter((r) => r.status === 'created').length;
  const failed = allResults.filter((r) => r.status === 'failed').length;

  return reply.code(failed > 0 ? 207 : 201).send({
    total: rows.length,
    created,
    failed,
    results: allResults,
  });
});

5. Debugging Narrative — Manoj's Experience

Manoj initially tried replacing the for...of loop with a single Promise.all over all 500 rows. This improved speed dramatically (from 45s to ~200ms) but caused a MongoDB connection pool exhaustion error — the Mongoose connection pool defaulted to 5 connections, and 500 simultaneous inserts overwhelmed it.

  1. First attempt: Promise.all(rows.map(...)) — fast but crashed with MongoPoolClearedError: Connection pool for db was cleared.
  2. Diagnosis: Mongoose logs showed 500 concurrent operations on a pool of 5 connections. The pool couldn't queue that many operations.
  3. Second attempt: Used insertMany instead — worked for valid rows, but one invalid row caused the entire batch to fail (no partial success reporting).
  4. Final solution: Chunked into batches of 50, used Promise.allSettled per chunk. This kept concurrent operations within pool limits while still reporting per-row outcomes. Also considered using p-map with concurrency: 10 for finer control.

The fix reduced import time from 45s to 1.2s and provided a detailed JSON report of which rows succeeded and which failed with specific error messages (e.g., "Duplicate email: john@example.com").

6. Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallMitigation
Sequential await in loops serializes inherently parallel operationsCollect promises into an array, use Promise.all or Promise.allSettled; chunk for pool-limited resources
Promise.all with hundreds of concurrent operations exhausts DB connection poolUse chunked batches (e.g., 50 at a time) or p-map with a concurrency limit; increase pool size if needed
Missing return in .then() chain causes subsequent .then to receive undefinedPrefer async/await over .then() chains; if using chains, ESLint rule promise/always-return catches missing returns
Fire-and-forget async calls (doSomethingAsync() without await) lose errors silentlyAdd no-floating-promises ESLint rule (from @typescript-eslint); require all promises to be awaited, returned, or explicitly voided

7. Validation Strategy

  • Performance benchmark: Import 500 valid contacts; assert completion time is under 3 seconds (was 45s before fix)
  • Partial failure test: Import CSV with 10 valid and 2 duplicate emails; assert HTTP 207 response with created: 10, failed: 2 and specific error messages
  • Connection pool test: Set Mongoose pool size to 5; import 500 contacts; assert no MongoPoolClearedError (chunking keeps concurrent ops within pool limits)
  • Playwright E2E: Upload a CSV file through the UI; verify the import summary shows correct counts and the contacts list reflects newly imported entries
